meta.title: Projectile Range: Why 45° is the Sweet Spot
meta.source: Projectile Range Problem — v₀ = 20 m/s, θ = 45°, g = 9.8 m/s²

problem.text: A ball is launched at $\theta = 45°$ with initial speed $v_0 = 20$ m/s from flat ground. Assuming no air resistance and $g = 9.8$ m/s², find the horizontal range $R$ — the distance from launch to landing.
problem.highlight: Find the range R of a projectile launched at 45° with v₀ = 20 m/s

viz_requirements.description: A 2D coordinate plane in landscape orientation. Origin at lower-left. X-axis is the ground (labeled 'horizontal distance (m)'). Y-axis points upward (labeled 'height (m)'). Faint grid lines. World coordinates ~50 m wide × 30 m tall. Color palette: white background, dark axes. Gold = trajectory/key results, Green = horizontal components, Blue = vertical components, Red = gravity label. A small ball icon sits at the origin. All scene objects animate in sync with narration beats, with a show-before-tell rule: visual fires 0.5–1 s before narrator names it. Between segments, non-essential elements fade to 15% opacity.

actions.description: Draw axes, faint grid, and axis labels. Place a small ball icon at the origin. Scene is empty except for axes — maximum focus on the setup.
Render (or reposition) the ball icon at given world coordinates.
Display a faint animated question mark floating above the ball to build curiosity before anything is revealed.
Grow a velocity arrow from the origin at the specified angle and speed. Arrow color, label, and animation duration are configurable.
Sweep a protractor arc from the x-axis to the velocity arrow, with an angle label. Fires after the velocity arrow is drawn.
Animate the decomposition of the diagonal gold arrow into a horizontal green arrow and a vertical blue arrow, forming a right triangle. Labels appear on each leg with component values.
Make a specific velocity component arrow pulse once to emphasize its independence.

Move the ball along a computed parabolic path in slow motion. Simultaneously render a live green horizontal arrow (constant length) and a live blue vertical arrow (shrinking, zeroing at apex, then growing downward) traveling with the ball.

Place a glowing dot at the trajectory apex. Drop a dashed vertical line from the apex to the x-axis. Show a label 'v_y = 0 at peak' near the dot. Keep the horizontal green arrow glowing to show the ball is still moving sideways.

Highlight the left half of the drawn arc in gold, then animate a mirror-fold so the right half draws itself as a ghost copy of the left. Both halves then pulse gold together to emphasize perfect symmetry.

Fade in a LaTeX equation block at a designated screen position. The 'step' parameter selects which equation: 'yOfT' shows y(t)=10√2·t−4.9t², 'setZero' appends =0, 'factored' morphs to t(10√2−4.9t)=0, 'horizontal' shows x(t)=10√2·t, 'generalExpanded' shows R=v₀cosθ·(2v₀sinθ/g).
Gray out the t=0 solution branch with a strikethrough and note 'launch'. Light up the nonzero solution t=10√2/4.9≈2.89 s in gold with a gentle glow.
Animate the flight time value substituting into the horizontal equation step by step: 10√2·(10√2/4.9) → 200/4.9 → 40.8 m. Each step appears sequentially, previous step dims to 30%.

Strike a dashed vertical line at x=range on the x-axis. Draw a bold horizontal bracket from x=0 to x=range along the ground. Label it 'R ≈ 40.8 m' in gold. The arc's landing point touches the dashed line exactly.
Draw a glowing box around the '2 sinθ cosθ' portion of the expanded range expression. Fade in the identity label '2sinθcosθ = sin(2θ)' above the box.
Animate the boxed '2sinθcosθ' morphing and compressing into 'sin(2θ)'. The final formula R = v₀²sin(2θ)/g appears in gold. This is a slow, deliberate 2-second animation.
Show the numeric verification: (400·sin90°)/9.8 → 400/9.8 → 40.8 m. A green checkmark appears after the final value. Previous equation steps dim to 30% as each new step appears.
Draw all arcs simultaneously from the origin at specified angles in gray. Then redraw the 45° arc in gold, slightly thicker. Place landing dots on the x-axis for each arc. The 40.8 m dot pulses gold.

Slide in a graph panel inset on the right side of the scene. Draw the sin(2θ) curve from 0° to 90° with θ on x-axis and R on y-axis. Map each fan arc's landing distance to a dot on the curve one by one. Highlight the peak at θ=45° with a gold star.

Zoom out smoothly to show both the fan of arcs and the sine curve graph side by side in full. The 45° arc and the curve's peak glow together in gold. A final label appears: 'R_max = v₀²/g ≈ 40.8 m at θ = 45°'.
Fade all scene elements to a specified low opacity except for the listed element IDs, to focus viewer attention.
Fade non-essential elements from the previous segment to 15% opacity and prepare the scene for the next segment's first visual beat.

nodes.title: The Setup — A Simple Question
nodes.objective: Establish the problem visually and build curiosity about 2D projectile motion.
nodes.context_from_previous: This is the opening act. The student sees a blank coordinate plane and a ball at the origin for the first time.

beat_outline.narration_hint: Imagine throwing a ball at exactly 45 degrees. It arcs through the air and lands somewhere. The question: how far away?
We launch with a speed of 20 meters per second, at an angle of 45 degrees above the ground.
The ball is moving at an angle — not just sideways, not just upward. Both at once. That's what makes this interesting.

nodes.title: Splitting the Arrow — Horizontal & Vertical
nodes.objective: Show that diagonal velocity = horizontal component + vertical component, and that these evolve independently.
nodes.context_from_previous: Student sees the gold velocity arrow at 45° from the origin, with the angle arc labeled. Nothing else is on screen.

beat_outline.narration_hint: Any velocity at an angle secretly contains two independent pieces. Let's pull them apart.
The horizontal piece — in green — is the sideways speed. It stays constant forever. Nothing pushes the ball sideways.
The vertical piece — in blue — is the upward speed. Gravity will fight this down to zero at the top, then reverse it.
Both components come from the same launch — cosine for horizontal, sine for vertical. At 45°, they're equal: both are 10 root 2 meters per second.

nodes.question_hint: A ball is launched at 45° with speed 20 m/s. What is the horizontal component of the initial velocity? (Enter exact or decimal value)
nodes.wrong_path_hint: The student likely confused the full launch speed with its horizontal component, or divided incorrectly. Revisit the vector triangle: the diagonal is the hypotenuse, not a leg.

wrong_path_acts.title: Vector Components — The Right Triangle Reminder
wrong_path_acts.objective: Correct the misconception that the launch speed equals the horizontal speed; reinforce SOH-CAH-TOA with the right-triangle visual.
wrong_path_acts.context_from_previous: Student answered incorrectly on the decomposition gate — likely gave 20 m/s or 10 m/s instead of 10√2 m/s.

beat_outline.narration_hint: Let's slow down. The hypotenuse of a right triangle is always longer than either leg. The 20 m/s is the hypotenuse — the total speed. The components are the legs.
Cosine gives adjacent over hypotenuse. So horizontal = 20·cos(45°) = 20·(√2/2) = 10√2. Sine gives opposite over hypotenuse. So vertical = 20·sin(45°) = 10√2.

nodes.title: Following the Flight — Live Velocity Vectors
nodes.objective: Build intuition for in-flight motion by watching velocity arrows evolve along the parabolic arc.
nodes.context_from_previous: Student understands horizontal = 10√2 m/s (green, constant) and vertical = 10√2 m/s upward (blue, changing). The right-triangle decomposition is visible on screen.

beat_outline.narration_hint: Let's watch the ball fly. The horizontal arrow never changes — nothing pushes it sideways.
But the vertical arrow shrinks as gravity decelerates the ball upward. At the very top, the vertical velocity hits zero.
Then gravity takes over — the ball accelerates downward. The vertical arrow flips and grows. Meanwhile the green horizontal arrow has been constant the whole time.

nodes.title: Symmetry & the Landing Clock
nodes.objective: Use the arc's left-right symmetry to motivate setting y(t) = 0, then solve for total flight time.
nodes.context_from_previous: The full parabolic arc is drawn on screen. Apex dot and dashed vertical line are visible. The ball has landed at y = 0 at the arc's right end.

beat_outline.narration_hint: Look at this arc. The left half and right half are perfect mirror images. Whatever the ball does going up, it undoes on the way down.
To find total flight time, we ask: when does the ball return to ground level? That means y of t equals zero.
Factor out t: we get t times the quantity 10 root 2 minus 4.9t equals zero. Two solutions.
t equals zero is the launch — not useful. The second solution is our flight time: t equals 10 root 2 over 4.9, approximately 2.89 seconds.

nodes.question_hint: Why do we set y(t) = 0 to find the time of flight?
nodes.wrong_path_hint: Student likely confused the landing condition with the apex condition (velocity = 0) or another misconception. Show two distinct visual moments: apex vs. landing.

wrong_path_acts.title: Apex vs. Landing — Two Very Different Moments
wrong_path_acts.objective: Visually distinguish y = 0 (landing) from v_y = 0 (apex) to clear up the most common confusion.
wrong_path_acts.context_from_previous: Student incorrectly chose 'velocity is zero at that point' or another wrong answer for the landing condition gate.

beat_outline.narration_hint: There's a crucial difference between two moments in flight. Let's see both side by side.
At the apex: the ball is at maximum height — y is at its largest. The vertical velocity is zero, but the ball is still in the air and still moving horizontally.
At landing: y equals zero — the ball is back on the ground. Velocity points downward at an angle. These are completely different events. y equals zero means the ball has returned to earth.

nodes.title: Computing the Range — Time Meets Distance
nodes.objective: Plug flight time into the horizontal equation to compute the numerical range R ≈ 40.8 m.
nodes.context_from_previous: Student knows flight time t = 10√2/4.9 ≈ 2.89 s (glowing gold on screen). Parabolic arc is drawn. Non-essential elements faded.

beat_outline.narration_hint: Now the horizontal equation. Constant speed, no acceleration. Distance equals speed times time.
We substitute our flight time in: 10 root 2 times 10 root 2 over 4.9. Watch how the roots cancel beautifully.
10 root 2 times 10 root 2 is 100 times 2 — that's 200. Divided by 4.9 gives us approximately 40.8 meters.

nodes.title: The Beautiful Compression — sin(2θ) Emerges
nodes.objective: Derive the general range formula R = v₀²sin(2θ)/g by recognizing the double-angle identity in the algebra.
nodes.context_from_previous: Student has computed R ≈ 40.8 m numerically. The range bracket and arc are visible on screen. The narration transitions: 'there's something more beautiful hiding here.'

beat_outline.narration_hint: Instead of numbers, let's use symbols. The range for any launch angle θ and any speed v₀ is: horizontal velocity times flight time. That's v₀ cosθ times 2v₀ sinθ over g.
Look at the trig terms: cosθ and sinθ sitting next to each other, multiplied by 2. That pattern has a name.
The double-angle identity: 2 sinθ cosθ equals sin of 2θ. These two separate trig functions literally compress into one.
And let's verify with our numbers: 400 times sin 90° divided by 9.8 equals 400 over 9.8 — the same 40.8 meters. It checks out perfectly.

nodes.question_hint: Using R = v₀²sin(2θ)/g, find the range for a ball launched at 30° with v₀ = 20 m/s and g = 9.8 m/s². Round to one decimal place.
nodes.wrong_path_hint: Student likely used sin(30°) instead of sin(2·30°=60°) — the classic double-angle mistake — or plugged in θ=45° again. Replay the sin(2θ) identity animation with emphasis on the factor of 2.

wrong_path_acts.title: The Double-Angle — Don't Forget the 2
wrong_path_acts.objective: Correct the mistake of using sin(θ) instead of sin(2θ) in the range formula.
wrong_path_acts.context_from_previous: Student gave 20.4 m (used sin30°) or 40.8 m (used θ=45° again) instead of the correct 35.4 m.

beat_outline.narration_hint: Here's the key: the argument inside the sine is 2 times theta — not theta alone. Let's make that crystal clear.
For theta equals 30 degrees: 2 times 30 is 60. So we need sin of 60 degrees — which is root 3 over 2 — NOT sin of 30.

nodes.title: The Universe Chose 45° — The Visual Climax
nodes.objective: Reveal why 45° maximizes range using the fan of trajectories and the sin(2θ) curve — the emotional and visual payoff.
nodes.context_from_previous: Student has the full formula R = v₀²sin(2θ)/g glowing in gold. The verified numerical result (40.8 m) is confirmed. This is the climax act.

beat_outline.narration_hint: Our formula contains sin(2θ). A sine function reaches its maximum — the value 1 — when its argument equals 90 degrees. So maximum range happens when 2θ = 90°, which means θ = 45°. Our problem secretly used the optimal angle.
Watch — every angle, same speed. The 45° arc reaches furthest. The others fall short. Symmetric pairs like 30° and 60° land at the same spot.
Now let's map these landing distances to the sin(2θ) curve. Each arc corresponds to one point on this graph.
The peak of the curve sits exactly at 45 degrees. That gold star is the answer to a deeper question: not just 'how far?' but 'what angle goes farthest?' And the universe says: 45°.
